title: practical english usage - Part 310
content:
For information about causative structures with make, ►     107.

(interesting) experience.

We   use take, not make, in take a photo, and have, not make, in have an I'll start on the vegetables as soon as I've done the beds.

He's old enough to make his own bed now.

the housework. Compare: Note that we  say make a bed, but we  often talk about doing the bed(s) as part of a profit, a fortune, love, peace, war, a bed, progress an effort, an excuse, an exception, a mistake, a noise, a phone call, money, make a journey, an offer, arrangements, a suggestion, a decision, an attempt, one's hair, one's teeth, one's duty, 50mph do good, harm, business, one's best, somebody a favour, sport, some exercise, 6 common       fixed    expressions ‘ What are we going to eat? ‘ Well, I could do an omelette.' activity - as if we  are not claiming to produce any very special results.

We   sometimes use do in place of make, to sound casual about a creative 5 do    instead      of  make My father and I once made a boat.

I've Just made a cake.  Let's make a plan.

We   often use make to talk about constructing, building, creating, etc.

4 make      for   constructing,         creating,      etc Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary  •  438   drown We have had to postpone the meeting(,) due/owing to the strike.

Due/Owing to the bad weather(,) the match was cancelled.

sentence by a comma.

Phrases beginning due/owing to are often separated from the rest of their than owing to.

Due to and owing to are similar to ‘ because of. Due to is more common 439 due     t o  and   owing       to  ___________________ He (was) drowned while trying to swim across a river.

accidental drowning.

Both active and passive forms of drown are common when we  talk about 438 drown Note also the expression well dressed.

She always dresses in green. He dresses well.

The  active form dress (in) can be used to give the idea of repetition or habit.

She had on orange pajamas. (AmE) She was wearing orange pyjamas.

Be wearing and have on (especially AmE)  are also very common.

She was dressed in orange pyjamas.

not                                              or (     . .. dressed with-...  . . .dressing in ...) / didn’t recognise him  because he was dressed in a dark suit.

form be dressed in (note the preposition).

To  say what somebody is/was wearing on a particular occasion, we  can use the 3 verb:   wearing      clothes Can you take Jack’s boots off for him?

I put on a sweater, but it was so warm that I had to take it off again.

Put on and take off are generally used when clothes are mentioned.

Get dressed and come downstairs at once!

undressing oneself.

In informal English, we  use get dressed/undressed to talk about dressing or I'm going to undress in front of the fire.

Could you dress the children for me?

It only takes me five minutes to dress in the morning.

else. Undress is used for taking clothes off.

The  verb dress can be used to talk about putting clothes on oneself or somebody 2 verb:   putting      clothes      on not He looks good in evening dress. (     ... in an evening dress.) evening dress, battledress).

used mostly to talk about special kinds of clothing (for example national dress, It means 'clothing’ , 'clothes’ . It is not very common in modern English, and is There is also an uncountable noun dress (not used with the article a/an).

vocabulary •  442  east and eastern, north and northern, etc North Carolina - the northern United States South Africa (a country) - southern Africa (an area) the south coast - the southern counties of Britain the north side of the house - the northern part of the country (e.g. the names of countries or states). Compare: indefinite or larger areas, and east, north, etc for more clearly defined places We   often prefer eastern, northern, etc when we  are talking about vague, the difference 1 adjectives: 442 east     an  d  eas   tern,     n orth     and   norther       n,  etc ►    591 early,     soon     and   quickly I met them during my stay in China.

I'll try to phone you during the meeting. (     ... in the meeting.) not (     ... in his military service.) He had some strange experiences during his military service.
